step1.title: Hi! Meet your AI team
step1.body: Seven roles — team-lead, backend, frontend, QA, architect, DevOps, tech-writer — collaborate on tasks as a single team. A quick minute to show you how it works.

step2.title: Tasks flow through columns
step2.body: Tasks move from «To do» → «In progress» → «Review» → «Done». Each column reflects the current state of work.

step3.title: Click a card for details
step3.body: Clicking a task card opens its details. Coloured chips show the priority, assigned role, and the LLM model being used.

step4.title: Create tasks yourself
step4.body: Click «+ New task», describe what needs doing. The team-lead will break it down and delegate.

step5.title: Inbox — your action queue
step5.body: Everything that needs your attention lands here: approvals, team questions, and work ready to be signed off.

step6.title: Statistics — team metrics
step6.body: Token usage, model costs, activity heatmap — everything about how the team is spending its time and budget.

step7.title: Roles — your team roster
step7.body: See who is on your team. You can add custom roles or edit existing ones — each role is a markdown system prompt.

step8.title: Archive — completed work
step8.body: Done tasks older than 7 days live here. Nothing is deleted — the history is always accessible.

step9.title: Settings — language, theme, team
step9.body: Change the interface language, colour theme, auto-mode limits, and team-lead expertise level.

step10.title: Launch the team-lead
step10.body: ▶ Start team — the team-lead reads the board, picks the next task and starts delegating. «⏹ Stop» ends the session.

step11.title: Auto mode
step11.body: 🤖 Auto — the team-lead starts the next session on their own whenever there are tasks in the queue. No need to click «Start» every time.

step12.title: Chat with the team-lead
step12.body: Leave messages here — the team-lead reads them at the start of each session and reports back. This is your async communication channel.

intro.title: Introduction
intro.body:
What is Devboard

Devboard is a local AI development team that runs on your own computer. Not in the cloud, not on someone else's server — yours. You hire seven virtual specialists: team-lead, backend, QA, architect, frontend, DevOps, and tech-writer. You write tasks; they do the work.

The key difference from ChatGPT or Copilot: this is a team, not a single assistant. The team has a persistent task board — history carries over between sessions. The team-lead knows what happened yesterday, which tasks are in progress, what is stuck. This is not a one-off chat — it is a process.

Example: you want to build a landing page. You create one task: «Build landing page for product X». The team-lead breaks it down — frontend writes the HTML/CSS, backend wires up the contact form, tech-writer drafts the copy. You review and accept the result. All of this without you being involved in the work itself.

7 team roles

👔 Team Lead

What they do: reads the board, breaks down tasks, delegates to roles, makes priority calls. The entry point — always runs first.
Typical task: «Decompose "Add chat" and create subtasks for backend and frontend».
Model: claude-opus — the most capable, needed for complex decisions and coordination.

⚙️ Backend

What they do: writes server-side Python code, works with the database, REST APIs, integrations.
Typical task: «Add GET /api/tasks/export that returns a CSV file».
Model: claude-sonnet — fast and accurate for code.

🔍 QA

What they do: writes and runs tests, finds bugs, files regression tasks.
Typical task: «Verify the new endpoint handles an empty list correctly».
Model: claude-sonnet — sufficient for tests and analysis.

🏛️ Architect

What they do: designs system structure, writes ADRs (Architecture Decision Records), reviews solutions.
Typical task: «Write ADR-007 on choosing between SQLite and PostgreSQL».
Model: claude-opus — depth needed for architectural decisions.

🎨 Frontend

What they do: writes HTML/CSS/JavaScript, builds interfaces, makes pages responsive.
Typical task: «Add a dark mode toggle to the site header».
Model: claude-sonnet — handles UI tasks efficiently.

🛠️ DevOps

What they do: sets up Docker, CI/CD pipelines, deployments, monitoring, security.
Typical task: «Write a Dockerfile for the Flask app with gunicorn».
Model: claude-sonnet — standard configs do not need the heavy model.

📝 Tech Writer

What they do: writes documentation, README, CHANGELOG, user guides.
Typical task: «Write the Getting Started section for the README».
Model: claude-sonnet — sufficient for technical writing.

Task lifecycle

You created a task
        ↓
     todo  (in queue — waiting for team-lead)
        ↓  team-lead picks it up
      wip  (in progress with a role)
        ↓  role finishes the work
   review  (awaiting your sign-off)
        ↓  you click «✓ Accept»
      done  (closed)

If the team-lead needs your input, the task moves to needs_approval and appears in Inbox. If a task is waiting on another task, it is blocked. You see all of this on the board in real time.

What Devboard does not do

  • Does not run code in production. It writes code and files on your computer — you handle the deploy.
  • Does not send emails or make calls. No external communication without your involvement.
  • Does not pay for API itself. Tokens are consumed from your Claude subscription.
  • Does not remember context without the board. Anything not written into a task is lost between sessions.
  • Does not make decisions for you. Final accept is always yours. The team cannot close a task without your approval.

tasks.title: How to write tasks
tasks.body:
Three parts of a good task

Every task has three parts: Title, Description, and Acceptance criteria. The team-lead reads all three — that is the only input they have. If anything is unclear, they will ask a question and the task stalls. Writing clearly upfront saves you a day.

Example 1 — UI bug

❌ Bad

Title: fix bug
Description: something is off with the dates
Acceptance: it should work

Why this fails: the team-lead does not know where the bug is (which page?), what exactly is wrong with the dates (display? saving? format?), or when it reproduces. «It should work» cannot be verified — it is not a criterion. The team-lead will ask at least three follow-up questions and the task stalls for a day.

✅ Good

Title: Fix date display in task card
Description: On the board, the created-date in a task card shows «1970-01-01» instead of the real date. Reproduces for all tasks created before May 10. File: dashboard/static/app.js, function renderCard().
Acceptance:

  • Date in the card is displayed as dd.mm.yyyy
  • Old tasks (before May 10) also show the correct date
  • A test for renderCard() is added or updated

Why this works: the team-lead sees the page (board), the symptom (1970-01-01), the condition (old tasks), even the file and function. Acceptance is verifiable: open the board and check.

Example 2 — New feature

❌ Bad

Title: add chat
Description: I want there to be a chat
Acceptance: chat works

Why this fails: «chat» is a huge feature. The team-lead does not know: between whom, whether history is needed, how to store messages, what the UI should look like. They might build anything from a single input field to a full messenger. The result will almost certainly not match what you had in mind.

✅ Good

Title: Add /chat page with message history between user and team-lead
Description: Need a /chat page where the user leaves text messages for the team-lead and sees replies. History is stored in the DB and visible on reload. Design — mirror the existing chat panel in the right sidebar (already exists), just move it to a dedicated page.
Acceptance:

  • /chat opens without errors
  • Input field and Send button present
  • Messages are saved in the DB (table chat_messages)
  • History is visible after page reload
  • Team-lead sees messages on the next run

Why this works: description gives context (the chat panel already exists — just move it), acceptance is split into verifiable steps. Frontend has a design reference, backend knows the DB table.

Example 3 — Documentation

❌ Bad

Title: write docs
Description: we need documentation
Acceptance: docs are ready

Why this fails: «docs» could mean anything — README, API reference, a video script, a user guide. What language? For whom — a developer or an end user? The tech-writer has no starting point and will likely produce something generic that does not fit the actual need.

✅ Good

Title: Write Installation section in README with working command examples
Description: README.md is missing an installation section. Need a ## Installation section covering: cloning the repo, installing dependencies, running the app. Audience: a developer finding the project on GitHub for the first time. Language: English. Command examples must actually work.
Acceptance:

  • README.md has a ## Installation section
  • Section includes at least 3 commands (clone, install, run)
  • Commands are tested and work in a clean environment
  • No marketing language — just concrete steps

Why this works: specific file (README.md), specific section (## Installation), audience (GitHub developer), language, and a requirement that examples actually run. Acceptance is measurable.

Example 4 — Business logic

❌ Bad

Title: add payments
Description: we need a payment system
Acceptance: payments work

Why this fails: «payments» is critically underspecified. Which provider (Stripe? PayPal? something local)? Which plans? What happens after payment — account activation? Webhook? Refunds? Without this the backend might spend a day on Stripe while you were expecting a different provider. Also: where do the API keys come from?

✅ Good

Title: Add Stripe Checkout for Pro plan ($19/month)
Description: Need a «Buy Pro» button on the /pricing page. On click — redirect to Stripe Checkout. After payment Stripe calls the webhook /api/billing/webhook, which sets plan='pro' in the users table. Stripe keys (publishable + secret) are already in .env as STRIPE_PK and STRIPE_SK.
Acceptance:

  • «Buy Pro» button on /pricing redirects to Stripe Checkout
  • After successful payment users.plan='pro' is updated in the DB
  • Webhook handles payment_intent.succeeded events
  • A test for the webhook handler is written using a Stripe mock

Why this works: provider named (Stripe), plan and price stated, the full flow described (button → checkout → webhook → DB), keys already exist in .env, acceptance is verifiable.

Example 5 — Analytics

❌ Bad

Title: show user statistics
Description: I want to see who is active
Acceptance: statistics are there

Why this fails: «active» is subjective. Logged in once this year? Created a task this month? Started a session this week? The backend will implement their own definition of active, and the result will not match what you wanted. No information on where to display it (new page? existing stats?), no response format.

✅ Good

Title: Add GET /api/users/active returning MAU for the last 30 days
Description: Need GET /api/users/active?days=30 returning JSON with the count of unique users who had at least one event (task created or session started) in the last N days. The days param is optional, default 30. Display this number on the /stats page in the «Activity» block.
Acceptance:

  • GET /api/users/active returns {"active_users": N, "period_days": 30}
  • ?days=7 returns data for the last 7 days
  • The /stats page «Activity» block shows this number
  • Query runs in under 200ms on a DB with 10,000 events

Why this works: endpoint is exact, response format is specified (JSON with field names), the definition of «active» is given (at least one event), display location is named, there is a performance requirement.

What to do when a task is stuck

  1. Open the task and read the history. The team-lead often leaves a comment explaining the delay or asking a question.
  2. Team-lead asked a question? The task moved to «Needs approval» and appeared in your Inbox. Answer right in the task — the team-lead will see it on the next run.
  3. Task is in review? That means work is done and is waiting for you. Open the task, check the result. Click «✓ Accept» if it is good, or «↻ Rework» with an explanation of what needs fixing.
  4. Task is blocked? It is waiting for another task. Open the dependencies panel in the card — it shows what is blocking it. You may need to complete the blocking task first.
  5. Task stuck in wip with no activity? Start a session manually with «▶ Start team» — the team-lead will check the status and either continue or report the problem.

departments.title: How to create departments
departments.body:
When do you need a new department?

When your work goes beyond software development.

  • Marketing — blog posts, social content, audience research.
  • Design — banners, illustrations, landing pages.
  • Support — answering user questions and handling tickets.

When you do NOT need a new department

  • If the task is development work — use the existing dev department.
  • If it is a one-off task — create it inside dev, not a separate department.

How to create a department

  1. Click + Department in the sidebar.
  2. A modal opens: enter a name, description, and optionally pick a template.
  3. HR proposes a team plan. Edit it, then activate.
  4. Once activated, the department appears in the sidebar.

Available templates

Five ready-to-use templates: Marketing, Design, Sales, Support, Operations. Start from a template and tell HR "remove X, add Y" to customise.

hr.title: Working with HR role
hr.body:
Why a good brief matters

HR is smart, but not a mind-reader. The more clearly you describe the department, the better the team plan you get.

Good brief

Department name: Marketing
Description: Content team for a developer-focused startup. Audience: Russian-speaking developers, 25–35. Tone: friendly, technical. Output: blog posts (1500–2500 words), Twitter/LinkedIn posts, SEO research. Out of scope: video production, graphic design.

Why this works: clear audience, tone, what we produce, and what we explicitly exclude.

Bad brief

Name: Marketing
Description: marketing

HR will generate a generic template-based team — not optimised for your actual use case.

How the edit loop works

HR proposes a plan → you respond with edits → HR revises → you activate. Common edit requests:

  • "Add a Brand Strategist"
  • "Remove Social Media — we are not on social"
  • "Rename Marketing Lead to Head of Growth"

shortcuts.title: Keyboard shortcuts
shortcuts.body:
Keyboard shortcuts available in the dashboard.

KeyAction
EscClose modal
Ctrl / Cmd + EnterSave task in editor
Ctrl / Cmd + KSearch coming soon

step2.title: Step 2: chat with HR
step2.answer_placeholder: Tell HR what to change or confirm the plan…
step2.first_hint: HR session started. Wait for HR to reply — this may take a few seconds.

field.name: Name
field.description_placeholder: What the department does: goals, audience, format…

errors.answer_failed: Failed to send message to HR.
errors.approve_failed: Activation failed. You can edit the plan and try again.
errors.session_lost: HR session lost — try creating the department again.
errors.aborted: HR session aborted. Close the window and try again.

topbar.auto_title: Auto mode: the team-lead starts the next session on their own when there's work

auto.paused: Auto mode on. Paused now: {reason}. Last hour: {n}

silence.title: Team-lead ended the session but left no summary
silence.label: team-lead is silent

router.title: Model the router will pick

column.review_title: Team finished, waiting for your sign-off
column.done_title: Closed tasks (older than 7 days go to Archive)

reply.placeholder: What do you want to tell the team…

rework.title: What to rework?
rework.placeholder: Describe what's off and what to change…

archive.title: Archive
